expectations.text: Triggers the design gate (Phase 0) due to ambiguity
expectations.evidence: Step 6 Trigger Evaluation: 'Spec mentions 2+ distinct features: payments, refunds (YES).' 'Spec involves external integrations: payment processors, credit card APIs (YES).' 'Spec is highly ambiguous: like credit cards and stuff, refunds maybe?, idk the details yet (YES — multiple ambiguities).' Decision: 'RUN the design gate. Multiple trigger conditions met.'

expectations.text: Asks clarifying questions about the payment system — does NOT assume specific technologies or providers
expectations.evidence: Step 0a Q1 asks about payment methods ('just credit cards, or also debit cards, bank transfers, or digital wallets?'). Q2 asks about integration ('Should this integrate with a real payment gateway (Stripe, Square, etc.) or be a self-contained module?'). Q3 asks about refund scope ('full refunds only, or also partial refunds?'). Questions do not assume Stripe or any specific technology — they present options.

expectations.text: Asks questions one at a time, not as a dump of 10 questions at once
expectations.evidence: Step 0a shows Q1, then user answers. Q2, then user answers. Q3, then user answers. Three questions asked sequentially, each with a user response before the next. Not a batch dump.

expectations.text: Does NOT start writing code or tests before clarifying the spec
expectations.evidence: The design gate (Step 6) runs before Phase 1 (framework detection), Phase 2 (decomposition), and Phase 4 (agent orchestration). No test files or implementation files are created until after the design summary is approved. Step 0d: 'HARD GATE passed' is the checkpoint before any code.

expectations.text: Proposes design approaches with trade-offs (e.g., Stripe vs custom, sync vs async processing)
expectations.evidence: Step 0b presents two trade-offs: Trade-off 1 has 3 options (direct Stripe, gateway abstraction, queue-based) with pros/cons for each. Trade-off 2 has 2 options (synchronous refunds vs. refund request queue) with pros/cons. User selects from each.

expectations.text: Waits for user approval of the design before decomposing into work units
expectations.evidence: Step 0c produces the design summary. Step 0d: 'Proceed with this design? [confirm/modify/cancel]. User confirms. Design summary stored in state under designSummary. HARD GATE passed.' Only after this does Step 8 (Phase 2 — Work Decomposition) begin.

claims: Design gate correctly triggers on maximally ambiguous spec with 'idk the details yet'
Three clarifying questions asked one-at-a-time with user response between each
Two architectural trade-offs presented with 2-3 options each and pros/cons
HARD GATE enforced: no code written until design approved by user
Design summary includes Components, Data Flow, Key Decisions, and Out of Scope sections
Vague informal language ('like credit cards and stuff') transformed into concrete requirements

suggestions: Consider testing what happens if the user says 'cancel' at the design gate — does the skill abort cleanly?

The trace shows 3 clarifying questions. The skill instruction says 'Limit: 5 questions max, asked 1 at a time.' Testing with a spec that needs more than 3 but fewer than 5 questions would exercise this limit.
Consider adding an assertion that the design summary explicitly lists Out of Scope items, which is valuable for ambiguous specs to prevent scope creep.
eval_feedback.overall: The with_skill run perfectly demonstrates the design gate for an ambiguous specification. All 6 assertions pass. The trace shows the full design gate flow: detect ambiguity, ask targeted questions one at a time, present architectural trade-offs with pros/cons, produce a formal design summary, and enforce the hard gate before any code. This eval validates the core value proposition of the design gate for underspecified requirements.
